next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 8

Owen left the Great Hall as soon as he decently could. Christmas had thinned Hogwarts without making it kind. The portraits on the way out watched from their gilded frames like informers, and a laugh from two landings up came down the stone stripped of any warmth it might once have had. Even the Hall, with its bewitched candles and the paper chains somebody had bullied into service again, looked less like a feast than a room trying to remember what feast meant. After the East Undercroft he had wanted one room in the castle that admitted, without theory, what people were for.

He took the service stair toward the kitchens. The air changed halfway down. Cold stone gave way to yeast, onions, damp wool steaming itself dry, and the sweet metallic tang of washed copper. By the time he ducked through the painting and into the kitchens proper his glasses would have fogged, if he had worn any. The room hit him with light and condensation. Huge pans hung over the ranges, every polished curve blotched white with steam. Holly had been jammed into cracks in the beams where it would do no structural harm. Someone had stuck cloves into the peels of two oranges and set them by the big proving drawer, so the whole place smelled faintly festive and mostly of soup.

Tallow stood on an upturned crate at the nearest stove, dipping a ladle, watching the soup sheet back into the pot, and muttering at an open cutlery drawer. He had tucked a blue pencil behind one singed ear and left a greasy thumbprint on the side of his own cheek. On the crate beside him lay three bent dessert spoons...
open_hooks_to_carry: Use Tallow's route and the recent ward disturbance to justify access to the undercroft now.
Describe the anchor stone as material evidence, not mystical mood alone.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...Snape, Owen thought.

He had not seen him. It was simpler than that: a headmaster lived in the listening parts of a castle whether he wanted to or not. Some forms of school authority were built out of notice before they were built out of punishment. If the Hall had just rung, somebody higher in the stone might have felt the note.

Owen forced himself up, legs stiff from crouching. He put the wand away. It hurt less when he was not trying to use it, though even in the pocket it seemed to sit wrong, heavier at one end than the other.

At the door he stopped and looked back once.

The scar held its place in the floor, faint as a crack seen through milk. It was not going to scrub out with water and house-elf vinegar. It was not a mess. It was evidence.

He left without going near the hospital wing.

He got to the cellar with nobody seeing him, or nobody stopping him, which was not the same thing. The dormitory was mostly empty. Hufflepuffs were still below, or at Pomfrey's, or carrying the story of the Hall from bench to bench. Owen did not take the notes out. He sat on the end of his bed with the robe stuck to the burn on his shoulder and his right palm giving little dry pulses under the old scar.

Every time he shut his eyes he saw the white seam in the flagstones and Martha looking at the chalk under his nail. The two things sat together in his head and would not sort into anything useful. He listened to footsteps go past in the corridor outside, each one sending a faint answer through the stone, and kept not knowing whether he wanted hers to stop at the door or keep going.
state_deltas_required: The manuscript has answered Owen's first scar, and Tallow's taboo against the old work conflicts with the need to see the deeper site.
The First Art's cosmic context becomes factual when Owen reaches the East Undercroft and the Hogwarts anchor stone.
